2615.name: Arjun Ranganathan
2615.title: Exact minimum co-degree bounds for tight Hamilton cycles
2615.abstract: <div style="word-break: normal; overflow-wrap: break-word; hyphens: auto; text-align: justify;">One of the earliest hypergraph extensions of Dirac's theorem is a conjecture of Katona and Kierstead from 1999, which asserts that for all uniformities $k \ge 3$, any $n$-vertex $k$-graph in which every $(k-1)$-set extends to at least $\frac{n-k+2}{2}$ edges contains a tight Hamilton cycle. R\
2615.bio: Arjun Ranganathan started his PhD at University College London in October 2024 under the supervision of Shoham Letzter. Prior to this, he obtained a BS-MS degree in mathematics from the Indian Institute of Science Education and Research, Pune.

2614.name: Zhihan Jin
2614.title: From small eigenvalues to large cuts and Chowla's cosine problem
2614.abstract: We prove that every graph with average degree $d$ and smallest adjacency eigenvalue $|\lambda_n| \le d^t$ contains a clique of size $d^{1-O(t)}$. A simple corollary of this yields the first polynomial bound for Chowla's cosine problem (1965): for every finite set $A$ of natural integers, the minimum of the cosine polynomial satisfies $\min_x \sum_{a \in A} \cos(ax) < -|A|^{-0.09}$. Another application makes significant progress on the problem of MaxCut in $H$-free graphs initiated by Erdős and Lovász in the 1970s. We show that every $m$-edge graph with no clique of size $m^{0.49}$ has a cut of size at least $m/2 + m^{0.5001}$.
2614.bio: Zhihan Jin is a final-year PhD student at the Mathematics Department, ETH Zürich, under the supervision of Prof. Benny Sudakov. His research interests lie broadly in extremal and probabilistic combinatorics, as well as its applications in theoretical computer science.

2613.name: Yiting Wang
2613.title: Hitting time results for Hamiltonicity
2613.abstract: Given a graph $G$, consider a random subgraph of $H$ of $G$ constructed by starting from the empty graph and iteratively adding edges according to a uniformly random ordering of $E(G)$ until the minimum degree of $H$ becomes 2. Is the resulting subgraph Hamiltonian with high probability? The celebrated result of Ajtai–Komlós–Szemerédi and Bollobás shows this is the case for $G = K_n$. What about sparser host graphs? In this talk, I will survey known results and present two of our recent results, one on $(n,d,\lambda)$-graph and another on bipartite Dirac graphs. Based on joint works with Yaobin Chen, Yu Chen and Seonghyuk Im.
2613.bio: Yiting Wang is currently a PhD student at Institute of Science and Technology Austria, working with Prof. Matthew Kwan. He is broadly interested in probabilistic and extremal combinatorics.

2612.name: Jiaxi Nie
2612.title: Hypergraph Ramsey numbers with quasipolynomial growth rate
2612.abstract: For a 3-uniform hypergraph (3-graph) $F$, let $r(F,n)$ be the smallest $N$ such that any $N$-vertex $F$-free 3-graph has an independent set of size $n$. We construct a $3$-graph $H_2$ with six vertices and five edges such that $r(H_2,n)=n^{\Theta(\log n)}$, and a more general family of $3$-graphs $F$ for which $r(F,n)=n^{\log^{\Theta(1)}(n)}$. These are the first examples of hypergraph Ramsey numbers $r(F,n)$ that are neither polynomial in $n$ nor at least exponential in $n$. This is joint work with Xiaoyu He, Logan Post, and Jacques Verstraëte
2612.bio: Jiaxi Nie is currently a Visiting Assistant Professor at the Georgia Institute of Technology. He was a Postdoctoral Researcher at the Shanghai Center for Mathematical Sciences from 2022 to 2024. He received his Ph.D. in Mathematics from the University of California, San Diego, in 2022. His main research interests lie in extremal combinatorics, including Ramsey problems, Turán problems, graph decomposition problems, and related topics. He has published 21 papers in journals such as J. Combin. Theory Ser. B, Combinatorial Theory, Sci. China Math., Random Structures Algorithms, Combin. Probab. Comput., SIAM J. Discrete Math., and J. Graph Theory.

2611.name: Jun Yan
2611.title: Ramsey numbers of trees
2611.abstract: Motivated by two simple lower bound constructions, Burr conjectured that if $T$ is a tree with bipartition class sizes $t_1\geq t_2$, then its Ramsey number $R(T)$ is $\max\{2t_1,t_1+2t_2\}-1$. This conjecture has since been disproved, although all known counterexamples have large maximum degrees. In a joint work with Richard Montgomery and Matías Pavez-Signé, we show that there is a constant $c>0$, such that Burr's conjecture holds for all $n$-vertex trees with maximum degree at most $cn$. In this talk, we will give an overview of this proof and survey some related problems.
2611.bio: Jun Yan will soon take up a postdoc position in University of Waterloo working with Luke Postle and Michelle Delcourt. He obtained his PhD from University of Warwick in 2025 under the supervision of Richard Montgomery.

2610.name: Jan Petr
2610.title: Cheeger-type inequalities for the second largest spectral gap from $1$ of the normalized Laplacian
2610.abstract:
We investigate the second largest spectral gap from 1 of the normalized Laplacian of a graph. This quantity appears in the study of the rate of convergence of a random walk to its stationary distribution, and has a connection to Ramanujan graphs. We relate it to the Cheeger and dual Cheeger constants. We also introduce a new Cheeger-type constant, for which we establish sharp inequalities analogous to the classical Cheeger inequalities.
Joint work with Lies Beers and Raffaella Mulas.
2610.bio: Jan Petr is a postdoctoral researcher at the University of Passau in the Discrete Mathematics group of Prof. Stefan Glock. He obtained his doctorate at the University of Cambridge under the supervision of Prof. Béla Bollobás.

2609.name: Andrea Freschi
2609.title: Ramsey number of a cycle versus a graph of given size
2609.abstract:
Let $C_k$ be the cycle on $k$ vertices and $H$ be an arbitrary graph on $m$ edges and no isolated vertices. Erdős, Faudree, Rousseau and Schelp conjectured that if $m$ is sufficiently large compared to $k$ then the Ramsey number $R(C_k,H)$ is at most $2m+\left\lfloor\frac{k-1}{2}\right\rfloor$. This is known to hold for $k$ even and for $k\in\{3,5\}$. In this talk, we present a resolution of all remaining cases, thus confirming the conjecture.
This is joint work with Stijn Cambie, Patryk Morawski, Kalina Petrova and Alexey Pokrovskiy.
2609.bio: Andrea Freschi obtained his PhD at the University of Birmingham (UK) under the supervision of Andrew Treglown. Since October 2024, he has been a postdoctoral researcher at the Alfréd Rényi Institute in Budapest (Hungary) under the supervision of János Pach and Gábor Tardos.

2601.name: Jørgen Bang-Jensen
2601.title: Results and open problems on dichromatic numbers of digraphs and linkages in (di)graphs
2601.abstract: The strong dichromatic number of a digraph $D$ is the minimum number of sets in a partition $V_1,V_2,\ldots,V_k$ of $V(D)$ with the property that each of the induced subdigraphs $D[V_i]$ is acyclic. This parameter has been studied intensively in the last decades. Recently we generalized the concept of dichromatic number to strong acyclic dichromatic number, where we seek a vertex partition as above but we also require that each of the bipartite digraphs induced by the arcs between $V_i$ and $V_j$ is acyclic for all distinct indices $i$, $j$. This new refinement of dichromatic number leads many interesting new questions. In particular, while the dichromatic number of a bipartite digraph is obviously at most $2$, for every integer $K$, there exist bipartite digraphs whose acyclic dichromatic number is at least $K$ and it is even NP-complete to decide whether a given bipartite digraph has acyclic dichromatic number $2$. We discuss other differences between this new parameter and the dichromatic number. One of these is that while it is NP-complete to decide whether a tournament is dichromatic number $2$, there exists a polynomial algorithm for finding an acyclic dicolouring using $2$ colours if one exists. We also discuss several variants of the well-known linkage problems for digraphs.
2601.bio: Jørgen Bang-Jensen is an Emeritus Professor of Algorithms in the Department of Mathematics and Computer Science (IMADA), University of Southern Denmark (SDU), Odense, Denmark. His research focuses on discrete mathematics, graph theory, combinatorial optimization and algorithms, with a particular emphasis on directed graphs. He has published more than $156$ papers and two monographs. He is the co-author (with Gregory Gutin) of the standard monograph Digraphs: Theory, Algorithms and Applications. This book has accumulated more than $4000$ citations on Google Scholar, making it one of the most influential texts in graph theory and discrete mathematics. Its first edition has been translated into Chinese.

2602.name: Gregory Gutin
2602.title: Hamiltonian oriented path and cycle discrepancy of digraphs
2602.abstract: Erdos (1963) initiated extensive graph discrepancy research on 2-edge-colored graphs. Gishboliner, Krivelevich, and Michaeli (JGT 2023) launched similar research on oriented graphs. They conjectured the following extension of Dirac's theorem: If $D$ is an oriented graph on $n \ge 3$ vertices with minimum degree $\delta(D) \ge n/ 2$, then $D$ contains a Hamilton oriented cycle with at least $\delta(D)$ arcs in the same direction. This conjecture was proved by Freschi and Lo (JCTB 2024) who posed an open problem to extend their result to an Ore-type condition. We propose two conjectures for such extensions and prove results which provide support to the conjectures. For maximization of the number of arcs in the same direction in Hamilton oriented cycles and paths of semicomplete multipartite digraphs and locally semicomplete digraphs, we obtain characterizations which lead to polynomial-time algorithms. Note that the maximization problem is NP-hard for some other generalizations of tournaments.
2602.bio: Gregory Gutin is a Professor in the Department of Computer Science, Royal Holloway University of London. His research interests include graph theory and algorithms, exact and approximation algorithms, combinatorial optimization, game theory, and access control in information security. In 2017, he was elected a member of Academia Europaea. He has over 300 papers, chapters and sections published in refereed journals, conference proceedings and books. His publications have over 14750 citations.

2603.name: Anders Yeo
2603.title: Judicious Partitions in Edge-Weighted Graphs with Bounded Maximum Weighted Degree
2603.abstract:
We are given an edge-weighted graph $G$ and want to find a $k$-partition of $V(G)$ minimizing the weight of the edges in the heaviest induced subgraph. That is, we want to find a partition $(V_1,V_2,\dots,V_k)$ minimizing $\max_{i=1}^k w(G[V_i])$. In our bounds we also take into account the weight $w(V_1,V_2,\dots,V_k)$ of the cut induced by the partition (i.e., the total weight of edges with endpoints in different parts) and show the existence of a partition satisfying tight bounds for both quantities simultaneously. We establish such tight bounds for the case $k=2$ and $k=3$. We also show that, in general, these results cannot be extended to $k \geq 4$ without introducing an additional lower-order term, and we propose a corresponding conjecture.
Moreover, we prove that there always exists a $k$-partition satisfying $\max \{ w(G[X_i]) : i \in [k] \} \leq \frac{w(G)}{k^2} + \frac{k - 1}{2k^2} \Delta_w(G)$, where $\Delta_w(G)$ denotes the maximum weighted degree of $G$. This bound is tight for every integer $k \geq 2$.
2603.bio: Anders Yeo is a Professor in the Department of Mathematics and Computer Science (IMADA) at the University of Southern Denmark (SDU) in Odense, Denmark. His research focuses on discrete mathematics, graph theory, combinatorial optimization, and algorithms, with a strong emphasis on graph theory and in particular on directed graphs and total domination in graphs. He has published over $220$ papers and $2$ monographs, both co-authored with Michael Henning, titled "Total domination in graphs" and "Transversals in Linear Uniform Hypergraphs".

2604.name: Allan Lo
2604.title: Compatible Hamilton cycles
2604.abstract: The renowned theorem of Dirac states that if $G$ is a graph with minimum degree at least $n/2$ then $G$ has a Hamilton cycle. A natural generalisation asks what properties of an edge-colouring of $G$ guarantee the existence of a properly edge-coloured Hamilton cycle in $G$. This concept can be further generalised as follows: an incompatibility system for $G$ is a set $\mathcal{F}$ of `forbidden' pairs of adjacent edges, that is, $\mathcal{F}\subseteq \{\{uv,vw\}\in \binom{E(G)}{2}\}$. A cycle in $G$ is then compatible if no two of its edges form a pair in $\mathcal{F}$. The system $\mathcal{F}$ is called $\mu n$-bounded if for all $v\in V(G)$ and $uv\in E(G)$, there are at most $\mu n$ pairs $\{uv,vw\} \in \mathcal{F}$. How small must $\mu$ be to guarantee the existence of a compatible Hamilton cycle in $G$? Krivelevich, Lee and Sudakov showed that $\mu=10^{-16}$ suffices (for $n$ large), while an example of Bollobás and Erdős shows that $\mu\leq 1/4$ is necessary. In this talk, we show that if $\delta (G) \ge (1/2+\varepsilon)n$, then we have $1/8 \le \mu \le 1/6$. This is joint work with Natalie Behague (Dublin City), Francesco Di Braccio (LSE) and Bertille Granet (Warwick).
2604.bio: Allan Lo is an Associate Professor in the School of Mathematics at the University of Birmingham, United Kingdom. His research focuses on graph theory and combinatorics with a strong emphasis on tiling and Hamilton cycles in graphs and hypergraphs.

2605.name: Louis DeBiasio
2605.title: The Hajnal-Szemerédi theorem for digraphs
2605.abstract: The Hajnal-Szemerédi theorem says that every graph $G$ has an equitable $(\Delta(G)+1)$-coloring. This is one of the cornerstone results in graph theory and has been applied in a wide variety of settings. In particular, it is a key ingredient in Komlós, Sárközy, and Szemerédi's proof of the Pósa-Seymour conjecture on powers of Hamiltonian cycles. If we attempt to generalize the Hajnal-Szemerédi theorem to the setting of digraphs, we suddenly face many choices. Should we replace maximum degree with maximum out-degree, or maximum total-degree, or maximum semi-degree, or something else? Should we replace equitable coloring with equitable acyclic coloring, or something more restrictive? What happens when we restrict our attention to oriented graphs? Which of these versions would be useful for proving an analogue of the Pósa-Seymour conjecture for directed/oriented graphs? For that matter, what is the correct analogue of the Pósa-Seymour conjecture for directed/oriented graphs? I will discuss our attempts to answer these questions and highlight what remains to be done. This is based on a body of joint work with Andrzej Czygrinow, Jie Han, Hal Kierstead, Allan Lo, Theo Molla, Simón Piga, and Andrew Treglown.
2605.bio: Louis DeBiasio is a Professor of Mathematics at Miami University in Oxford, Ohio. He is an Associate Editor of the journal Discrete Mathematics. His research focuses on finite and infinite Ramsey theory, as well as spanning structures in graphs using regularity and absorbing methods.

2606.name: Yifan Jing
2606.title: A Structure theorem for sets with doubling 4+δ
2606.abstract: A question of Green asks whether every finite set of integers $A$ with doubling constant $K$ must contain a subset $A^{\prime}$ of comparable size whose small doubling is explained by some explicit algebraic structure. This was previously understood in the regime $K < 4 - \delta$, by work of Eberhard, Green, and Manners, who showed that one can find such a subset $A^{\prime}$ with density at least $1/2 + \varepsilon$ inside a long arithmetic progression. In this talk, I will discuss our extension of this picture to the range $4+\delta$. We prove that $A$ contains a large subset $A^{\prime}$ such that either $A^{\prime}$ has density at least $1/2 - \varepsilon$ in a long arithmetic progression, or $A^{\prime}$ has density at least $1- \varepsilon$ in a two-dimensional arithmetic progression. The proof combines the arithmetic regularity lemma, geometry of numbers, Brunn--Minkowski-type inequalities, and the structure theory behind the Gowers $U^2$ -norm. This is joint work with Akshat Mudgal.
2606.bio: Yifan Jing is currently an assistant professor in the Department of Mathematics at The Ohio State University. His main research interests include additive combinatorics, graph theory, harmonic analysis, and group theory. He received the 2023 ICA Kirkman Award. His research results have been published in journals such as GAFA, Math. Ann., JLMS, TAMS, and J. ACM.

2607.name: Xiaoyu He
2607.title: Off-diagonal Ramsey numbers for hypergraphs
2607.abstract:
If $H$ is a $k$-uniform hypergraph, or a family of such hypergraphs, define $r(H, n)$ to be the smallest $N$ such that any $N$-vertex $k$-uniform hypergraph contains either a copy of $H$ or an independent set of size $n$. For most known examples of $H$, $r(H,n)$ is known to grow polynomially or exponentially in $n$, and a central question is to determine the exact conditions under which $r(H, n)$ is polynomial. This talk gives a high-level overview of recent progress on this problem, including the resolution of the $500$ polynomial-to-exponential problem of Erdos and Hajnal, a full characterization when $H$ has at most two tightly connected components, and an example of an $8$-vertex $3$-uniform hypergraph $H$ for which $r(H, n)$ grows exactly quasipolynomially.
Based on joint works with Ruben Ascoli, David Conlon, Jacob Fox, Benjamin Gunby, Dhruv Mubayi, Jiaxi Nie, Logan Post, Andrew Suk, Jacques Verstraete, and Hans Hung-Hsun Yu.
2607.bio: Xiaoyu He is a Richard A. Duke Assistant Professor in the School of Mathematics at Georgia Tech. He is broadly interested in extremal and probabilistic combinatorics and theoretical computer science, with particular emphasis on Ramsey theory and coding theory.

2608.name: Amedeo Sgueglia
2608.title: On Kotzig's conjecture in random graphs
2608.abstract:
In 1963, Anton Kotzig famously conjectured that $K_n$, the complete graph of order $n$ , where $n$ is even, can be decomposed into $n-1$ perfect matchings such that every pair of these matchings forms a Hamilton cycle. The problem is still wide open and here we consider a variant of it for the binomial random graph $G(n,p)$ . In fact, our main result is a very precise answer for the following counting problem: given any $k$ edge-disjoint perfect matchings $M_1, \dots, M_k$ of $K_n$, how many perfect matchings $M^*$ in $K_n$ have the property that $M^* \cup M_i$ forms a Hamilton cycle for each $i \in [k]$?
Joint work with Stefan Glock.
2608.bio: Amedeo Sgueglia is a postdoctoral researcher at the University of Passau in the Discrete Mathematics group led by Professor Stefan Glock and he is supported by the Alexander von Humboldt Foundation. He is broadly interested in extremal and probabilistic combinatorics.
